coredata.dc:description: A fast and effective technique is the need of the hour for the treatment of dye polluted wastewater to safeguard living beings and the environment. Photocatalysis treatment of dye contaminated wastewater has emerged to be an appealing approach. Here in the present report, novel nitrogen doped‑carbon dots (N-C-dots) are produced by simple pyrolysis approach at 300 °C for different time intervals of 1 h, 2 h and 3 h using garlic (Allium Sativum) for the degradation of crystal violet (CV) dye. The resulting N-C-dots have crystalline nature, nearly spherical with particle size ranging from 3.06 to 15.97 nm, and biocompatible. Also, these N-C-dots showed excellent capability as photocatalyst (0.06 g) by degrading 40 ppm of CV dye up to 99.13 % at pH = 5 under UV irradiations in just 80 min. The capture studies showed that superoxide ions and hydroxyl radicals are primary components in the photocatalytic degradation of CV dye. Furthermore, these N-C-dots exhibited the excellent reusability, stability and biocompatibility sequentially.

head.ce:title: Biogenic synthesis of novel N-doped carbon-dots photocatalyst for remediation of wastewater

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fast and effective technique is the need of the hour for the treatment of dye polluted wastewater to safeguard living beings and the environment. Photocatalysis treatment of dye contaminated wastewater has emerged to be an appealing approach. Here in the present report, novel nitrogen doped‑carbon dots (N-C-dots) are produced by simple pyrolysis approach at 300 °C for different time intervals of 1 h, 2 h and 3 h using garlic (Allium Sativum) for the degradation of crystal violet (CV) dye. The resulting N-C-dots have crystalline nature, nearly spherical with particle size ranging from 3.06 to 15.97 nm, and biocompatible. Also, these N-C-dots showed excellent capability as photocatalyst (0.06 g) by degrading 40 ppm of CV dye up to 99.13 % at pH = 5 under UV irradiations in just 80 min. The capture studies showed that superoxide ions and hydroxyl radicals are primary components in the photocatalytic degradation of CV dye. Furthermore, these N-C-dots exhibited the excellent reusability, stability and biocompatibility sequentially.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Global environmental pollution is rising in conjunction alongside the growth and expansion of several manufacturing industries. Aquatic life is negatively impacted by the effluents released from these industries, while terrestrial organisms are negatively impacted indirectly. Our ecology is harmed by the hazardous contaminants found in this industrial discharge, which include hazardous dyes, antibiotics, and other substances [1]. Due to their widespread production and consumption, synthetic dyes pose serious environmental and health concerns [2]. At present, 100,000 different kinds of dyes are manufactured each year, and the textile industries use approximately 3.6 × 107 kg of dyes yearly [3,4]. About 20 % of all dyes produced globally are wasted during the dying process and released in textile effluents [5]. These dyes once released into the environment, lingers there for extended periods of time and eventually poses a threat to both aquatic and terrestrial life [6]. Consequently, treatment of dye contaminated wastewater is important. Adsorption, biotreatments, nanofiltration, centrifugation, the activated sludge method are some of the methods that have been used to treat dye-polluted wastewater. However, these processes have certain drawbacks, like slow dye degradation, and high expense [7]. On the other hand, photocatalytic method is an appealing option for treatment of dye contaminated wastewater because majority of photocatalytic dye degradation processes are fast, economically feasible, involves no or minimal generation of hazardous by-products along with almost complete degradation of different dyes [8].

Natural precursors-based photocatalysts, particularly C-dots, are widely used presently to remediate dye contaminated wastewater. It is because natural sources such as rice husk, vegetable waste, plants parts etc. offer a secure, quickly & widely available, and eco-friendly source of carbon for fabricating of various carbon-based NMs for wastewater remediation [9]. In recent past, several attempts have been done for utilizing natural sources as the main precursor for synthesizing of C-dots for potential applications, including sensing, bio-imaging, photocatalysis, drug delivery, etc. [10–12]. Their small size, chemical stability, potential for functionalization, fluorescent behavior, aqueous solubility, simple fabrication procedure, broad excitation spectra, tunable and narrow emission spectra and are just a few of the exceptional characteristics of C-dots [13–15]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fabricated from pear juice employing a straightforward hydrothermal approach exhibited significant photocatalytic tendency when placed under the influence of visible light, allowing 99.5 % MB eradication within 130 min due to their outstanding absorption of light, electron transfer, and detachment of photoinduced charge carrier [16]. C-dots produced from citrus limetta pulp waste via a single-step pyrolysis procedure showed photocatalytic decontamination of MB dye upon visible irradiations [17].

In the present work, we have synthesized reusable, biocompatible and efficient N-C-dots photocatalyst from green precursor i.e., garlic (allium sativum) via a simple, feasible and cost-effective one-step pyrolysis route at 300 °C for different time intervals of 1 h, 2 h and 3 h for the photocatalytic decontamination of CV toxified wastewater. The synthesized N-C-dots effectively decomposed CV dye. The degradation of CV dye by N-C-dots is accelerated by •OH and O2 • − radicals in the photocatalytic pathway as revealed by capture experiments. Furthermore, C-dots synthesized by pyrolyzing allium sativum for 3 h exhibited maximum degradation (99.13 %) of 40 ppm CV dye, achieved within 80 min. Furthermore, the wheat seeds germination activity in the presence of as-synthesized N-C-dots revealed that these N-C-dots are biocompatible and non-toxic. The fundamental goal of the present research work is to investigate the probable reason for pyrolysis duration based photocatalytic characteristics of resultant N-C-dots. Moreover, minimal usage of any chemical (only liq. NH3 used in this study) during the synthesis process is one of the most interesting features of this study.
All the chemicals used are of analytical grade including liq. NH3 (SRL, 30 %), Na2EDTA (CDH, 99.5 %), tert-butanol (CDH, 99.5 %), and p-benzoquinone (Loba Chemie, 98.0 %).

In the present work, first of all, the biomass precursor i.e., garlic (allium sativum) is sliced into small pieces and dried in the sunlight for almost 48 h. Then the precursor is pyrolyzed at 300 °C in the muffle furnace for time intervals of 1 h, 2 h and 3 h.

After pyrolysis the obtained samples are grinded separately into very fine powder employing mortar and pestle. Then the resultant fine powders are dispersed in 400 mL of double distilled water in 500 mL beaker separately so as to obtain their aqueous dispersions with the inclusion of 7 mL of liq. NH3 as dopant. The aqueous mixture so obtained are agitated for 2 days at 1250 rpm. Then resultant mixtures are left undisturbed for next 5 h so that large particles undergo sedimentation and then subjected to centrifugation for 45 min at 5000 rpm. Then, the supernatants of these aqueous mixtures are taken in beakers and subjected to heating on the hot plate to evaporate H2O content. It resulted into the production of N-C-dots as demonstrated in Scheme 1 . The N-C-dots obtained from garlic are blackish and labeled as N-G1h, N-G2h and N-G3h, where ‘N' denotes the dopant i.e., nitrogen, ‘G' represents the precursor i.e., garlic and 1 h, 2 h & 3 h represents the pyrolysis times, respectively.

X-ray diffraction pattern of the obtained N-doped-C-dots are obtained utilizing a “Rigaku Ultima IV XRD having CuK as the X-ray radiations source”. Perkin Elmer FRONTIER with KBR pellets has been used to conduct FTIR for the examination of functional moieties in 500–4000 cm−1 region. SEM and HR-TEM from Thermo Scientific is employed to determine the morphology and particle size at high resolution. “The STR 500 Confocal Micro Raman Spectrometer” has been utilized to determine the percentage purity of resulting doped-C-dots. Utilizing a SHIMADZU UV-3600i Plus spectrophotometer, UV–Vis assessment is carried out to understand optical and photocatalytic properties. The photoluminescence (PL) study has been done with a HORIBA Fluoromax spectrofluorometer.

The photocatalytic efficacy of the resulting N-C-dots has been investigated by decontamination of CV dye solution. For photocatalytic degradation, a 300 W ultraviolet (UV) light source was employed. In double-deionized water, CV solution with a fixed ppm conc. was taken in a beaker and then known dose (0.02–0.08 g) of photocatalyst (N-C-dots) in 50 mL of the dye solution was introduced. For establishing equilibrium conditions, dye solution was at first maintained in the dark and simultaneously stirred for 30 min. The dispersed solution was then irradiated by UV light for 80 min, with 3 mL of dye solution being taken out after every 20 min to collect the UV–vis absorption data for further investigations. The highest intensity of CV dye in UV–vis spectra was reported at 590 nm.

XRD pattern of resulting samples from garlic showed sharp peaks at 28.41o, 50.19o and 40.67o which are associated with (002), (102) and (100) planes of sp2 hybrid graphitic carbon atoms (Fig. 1 ). The peaks at 58.71o, 58.79o and 66.31o, 66.48o linked to (600) and (220) planes of diamond. This data is in close resemblance with the CQDs produced from extract of Mexican Mint [18]. While remaining peaks at 2θ of 21.36o, 29.76o, 35.76o, 37.78o, 43.47o, 46.76o and 73.77o are attributed to the planes (002), (202), (311), (200), (101), (400), (215), respectively [19–21]. It has been noticed that with rise in the pyrolysis interval, the number of peaks as well as their intensity increased. This indicated that with longer pyrolysis time, degree of crystalline nature also increased for the synthesized N-C-dots. N-G3h exhibited the tallest sharp XRD peak which is positioned at 2θ = 30.82° corresponding to plane (211) having d-spacing of 0.289 nm [18,22–25].

From XRD pattern of the samples, and using Eq. 1, the percentage crystallinity of the as-synthesized samples has been calculated [26–28].
(1)
Percentage Crystallinity =
A CP
A AP
× 100
where, ACP and AAP represent area under crystalline peaks and area under all the peaks in XRD pattern of synthesized NMs [29,30]. The % crystalline nature of N-G1h, N-G2h and N-G3h has been calculated to be 61.99 %, 65.96 % and 79.73 %, respectively.

To study the functional groups and chemical composition of the synthesized N-C-dots, XPS and FTIR spectroscopy were employed. As displayed in FTIR spectra, peaks in 3351–3391 cm−1 region are ascribed to –OH stretching and NH [23]; the peaks at 2168–2170 cm−1 denotes overtone due to C–H; the peaks 1593–1653 cm−1 represents alkene group [35]. While peaks around 1395–1404, 1116 and 1017–1027 cm−1 and are due to stretching of CN [36], asymmetric and symmetric C–O–C bending, sequentially [18,23,37]. Furthermore, the peaks ranging from 736 to 760 and 593–612 cm−1 have been ascribed to “aromatic CH out of the plane deformation/C-S stretching vibration [36,38] and aromatic sp2 CH bending” in N-C-dots, sequentially (Fig. 4 ) [25].

Fabricated N-C-dots contain of C, O, N, and S elements, as revealed by XPS survey spectra of N-G2h and N-G3h in Figs. 5 and 6
. XPS C1s spectra of N-G2h and N-G3h (Fig. 5(b) & 6(b)) can be split into 4 peaks having binding energies ranging from 282.30 to 282.45, 285.08–285.31, 290.19–290.20 and 292.90–292.96 eV, associated with carbon‑hydrogen bond [39], C-S/C=C/C-N [40–42], H–O–C=O or CO [43,44], and π-electrons free movement [42], sequentially. The O1s spectrum is resolved into 2 peaks in the range 528.42–528.48 and 529.64–529.94 eV that are assigned to ketone [36] and HO-C [40] as depicted in Figs. 5(c) and 6(c). N1s spectra is split into 3 peaks in the range 397.82–397.83, 398.36–398.60, and 399.60–399.62 eV are attributable to the existence of C-N-C [44], C-NH2 [42], and NH [36] (Figs. 5(d) & 6(d)), correspondingly. According to the aforementioned study, the synthesized C-dots may contain functional moieties like ketones, carboxylic acid, hydroxyl, and -NH, while S and N elements were additionally introduced into N-C-dots.

PL aids to comprehend the electronic makeup of N-C-dots. Fig. 7 displays the adjustable emissive characteristic, or PL-spectra, of aqueous solution containing the sparingly water-soluble N-C-dots. As can be seen in Fig. 7 (a), with increase in λex from 320 to 400 nm, emission peaks of resulting C-dots redshifts from roughly 392 to 496 nm. It is significant to consider that C-dots are known for their “excitation-dependent emission photoluminescence properties” [45]. The intensity of emission peaks increases with increment in the λex upto 380 nm and then decreases. The maximum intensity of emission peak was observed at λex = 380 nm for N-G3h [46,47]. Although the exact reason behind the photoluminescence shown by C-dots is not still entirely clear. Some of the studies related to PL of C-dots have linked it with the chemical and physical composition of C-dots [48]. With longer pyrolysis synthesis interval, the emission spectra's intensity of the N-C-dots increased, as displayed in Fig. 7 (b). The enhanced conversion of precursor components into carbonized substances is the primary cause of rising emission intensity with increased pyrolysis duration. The N-G3h, produced at 3 h of pyrolysis interval, have the greatest emission intensity, suggesting that the 3 h pyrolysis reaction time has the largest quantum yield (Φ). Conversely, during shorter times, specifically 1 h, the whole carbonization phase is mild, leading to low QY. These results bear resemblance to the literature [32,49].

For pristine NMs (such as doped-CuO and NiO) and their composites, the most effective photocatalyst often exhibits a minimal PL intensity in comparison to other pristine NMs and their composites. In this kind of scenario, the minimal PL intensity often denotes the slowest rate of reintegration of holes and electrons. Thus, the photocatalyst's performance is increased by the slowest possible reintegration of charge carriers [1,50]. However, in our work, we have solely synthesized doped C-dots rather than composites. Additionally, N-G3h, the most effective photocatalyst, has the highest PL intensity because, according to a research study by Deme et al., increased carbonization of the precursor occurs with longer pyrolysis times [32]. Furthermore, when compared to other synthesized doped-C-dots, N-G3h's minimum band gap value and smallest mean particle size justify its greatest photocatalytic efficacy. The alternative explanation could be that, according to the PL analysis, N-G3h has a comparatively fast charge carrier recombination rate. Thus, its photocatalytic efficacy ought to be the lowest. However, the effects of particle dimension and optical band gap values may have a greater influence on photocatalytic performance as compared to the impact of the PL intensity, which could have eventually increased the material's photocatalytic performance.

Every sample of N-C-dots has two distinct bands in its Raman spectrum: a wide D-band that indicates a disordered arrangement of sp3-hybridized carbon while comparatively narrower G-band that represents the neatly arranged sp2-hybridized carbon. D- and G-bands for N-G3h are situated at 1362 and 1559 cm−1, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 8 . This indicates that fabricated N-C-dots contain carbon nanoparticles (NPs).

Intensity ratio i.e., ID/IG ratio, that was found close to 0.99 (sp3/sp2) for N-G3h, indicates its greater percentage purity. In an analogous manner as shown in Table 2 , the purity of N-G1h and N-G2h is determined to be 0.76 and 0.86, sequentially. According to the Raman evaluations, the resulting N-C-dots seem to be pure [51]. Notably, the ID/IG ratio of 0.99 provides additional evidence of the exceptionally crystalline structure obtained during the formation of N-C-dots [52]. This is most likely related to amorphous carbonaceous substance obtained from carbonization of biomass having larger density defects.
The photocatalytic eradication and decolorization of CV dye is examined experimentally to study photocatalytic capability of carbon-dots in addition to optimization of several parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of pyrolysis duration on CV dye decomposition
ce:section.ce:para: N-G1h, N-G2h, N-G3h are utilized as photocatalysts for decontamination of CV dye. 0.05 g of N-C-dots are individually employed to decontaminate 50 mL CV dye having conc. of 30 ppm. The decrement in dye content is observed by a reduction in the absorption peak intensity (590 nm for CV dye) with time (0–30 min) in dark condition which is most probably due to adsorption of dye molecules by N-C-dots (Table 3 ). After 30 min in dark condition, no further decrement in the intensity of absorption peak was noticed. This indicated that the adsorption-desorption equilibria have attained.

Then the dye solution was exposed to UV light irradiations for 80 min. Under the influence of UV light irradiations, the obtained N-C-dots continuously reduced the CV dye concentration which revealed that these N-C-dots are excellent photocatalysts. It was found that N-G3h demonstrated greatest efficiency by initially adsorbing CV dye up to 39.76 % under dark condition (30 min) and then photocatalytically degrading 79.34 % remaining CV dye content within 80 min when subjected UV light irradiations as displayed in Fig. 9 (Table 3). Therefore, further investigations have been carried out employing N-G3h.

The significant likely origin of enhancing dye degradation efficacy of N-doped‑carbon-dots photocatalyst having longer pyrolyzed duration may be understood from HRTEM and UV–vis absorption studies. HRTEM examination revealed that particle size of obtained N-C-dots became smaller as pyrolysis gets longer. The extent of functional moieties lying on the surface in addition to overall surface area of resultant carbon-dots both became greater with reduction in particles size from N-G1h to N-G3h. Therefore, larger extent of dye molecules are possibly adsorbed on N-C-dots surface because of increase in overall surface area and because of existence of more number of surface-active functional moieties [53]. In addition, the band-gap of N-G3h is smallest among the obtained N-C-dots. Therefore, greater number of dye molecules were photocatalytically eradicated because of interaction between greater extent of surface-active functional moieties and adsorbed dye molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of dosages of N-G3h on photocatalytic dye decontamination
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalyst dosage is another important parameter that has a crucial role in photocatalytic dye degradation. The effect of photocatalyst dosage has been studies by adjusting amount of photocatalyst from 0.02 to 0.08 g. In the experiment procedure, 0.02–0.08 g of N-G3h, 50 mL of 30 ppm CV dye and contact time of 80 min, respectively, have been taken for examining the impact of photocatalyst amount on dye decontamination and discoloration. The % dye eradication increased with increase in N-G3h dosage and becomes maximum at 0.06 g of N-G3h, and then decreased as shown in Fig. 10 (a) and tabulated in Table 4 [53]. The degree of active sites on the N-C-dots surface influences its photocatalytic efficiency. Raising photocatalyst amount usually results in rise of both the overall area of surface and degree of surface functionalities. Both of these factors result in greater extent of dye decontamination. It is to be noted that when the N-G3h content rises from 0.06 g to 0.08 g, the % CV eradication was decreased. This outcome results from a reduction in the volume of suspension that is photoactivated due to an increase in photocatalyst amount that reduces UV-light penetration [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of dye concentration on photocatalytic dye decontamination
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the influence of dye conc., different CV dye solutions are prepared having conc. of 20, 30, 40 and 50 ppm. In the experimental procedure, 0.06 g of N-G3h, 50 mL of each dye solution with varying conc., and contact time of 80 min, respectively, have been utilized. It was found that with increment in the dye conc., the degree of % photocatalytic dye eradication firstly increased up to 40 ppm of CV dye conc. and then decreased as illustrated in Fig. 10 (b) and tabulated in Table 5 [55].

The highest % photocatalytic degradation of CV dye was observed at 40 ppm conc. as shown in Fig. 10 (b). The increased deterioration at 40 ppm could be the result of more CV molecules sticking to the catalyst's surface-active site, which would speed up the degradation process. However, the % breakdown of CV dye seems to decline as the dye concentration rises beyond 40 ppm, it is most likely due to the fact that the photocatalyst adsorbs greater amounts of dye at high concentrations, preventing the radiations from reaching the photocatalyst (the pathway length of incident photons gets shorter) and decreasing CV deterioration. Similar results were reported by Ahmed et al. [56] and Kalaycıoglu et al. [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Point of zero charge (pHpzc) of N-G3h and effect of pH on photocatalytic dye decontamination
ce:section.ce:para: Utilizing the concept of zero-point charge (pHpzc), the optimal pH for dye decontamination can be determined. If the pH of dye solution is below pHpzc, then photocatalyst's surface become positively charged. On the other flip, it become negatively charged when pH of dye solution is above pHpzc of photocatalyst [58]. Using salt addition method, the pHpzc of N-G3h was determined to be 3.58 as displayed in Fig. 11 (a) [19]. This implies that when the pH of dye solution is below 3.58, the N-G3h surface become positively charged and consequently, N-G3h and cationic CV dye are electrostatically repelled from each other, which caused small fraction of CV decontamination. On the other side, at pH of dye solution above 3.58, the photocatalyst surface become anionic and hence N-G3h adsorb greater number of CV molecules, consequently resulting into greater percentage of CV dye degradation.

The impact of pH has been examined by changing the pH of dye solution from 3 to 9 utilizing 0.1 M NaOH and HCl solution. In the experiment procedure, 0.06 g of N-G3h, 50 mL of 40 ppm CV, respectively, have been taken for investigating the impact of pH of dye solution on CV decontamination for a contact time of 80 min. It was observed that rising the pH of the dye solution from 3 to 5, the % dye eradication increased and becomes highest at pH = 5 and then decreased as displayed in Fig. 11 (b) and tabulated in Table 6 .

The adsorption of dye molecules by photocatalyst affects the dye decomposition capability of the photocatalyst. The zero-point charge of N-G3h was found to be 3.58. That means the surface of N-G3h will become negatively charged above this pH. The negative charge on the N-G3h also gets stronger at even higher pH. Because CV is typical cationic dye, the photocatalyst's negative charge consequently help to promote the adsorption (47.98 % of initial dye concentration at pH 5) of this dye and hence results in greater photocatalytic capability of N-G3h for CV dye degradation. The photocatalyst i.e., N-G3h exhibited maximum degradation of CV at pH = 5. But when the pH level is above 5 for CV dye solutions, the activity of N-G3h is reduced. It is probably due to competition among •OH radicals and molecules of dye as they try to bind to the negatively charged photocatalyst surface. Consequently, some of the •OH radicals do not participate in dye degradation process and leads to reduction in the dye eradication capability of the photocatalyst [59]. Another possibility is that at highly alkaline pH, there is greater degree of adsorption of cationic CV dye molecules on the intensively negatively charged surface of N-G3h which results into the formation of a thin film on the surface of the photocatalyst. This film functions as a barrier, preventing irradiated UV radiation from reaching the catalyst surface, leading to lesser dye degradation at higher pH values [60].

The degradation of CV dye employing N-G3h i.e., carbon-dots having the greatest photocatalytic capability is displayed in Fig. 12 (a) and (b), sequentially. The reduction in dye content is represented by decrease in the absorption peak (590 nm) intensity of with irradiations time (0–80 min). CV dye exhibited no degradation in the presence of solely light irradiations, however, 47.98 % adsorption of the CV dye was noticed under dark and optimized conditions (i.e., in existence of solely N-G3h) as displayed in Fig. 12 (a & b) [61,62]. Photocatalytic eradication of CV dye by N-G3h demonstrated the first-order reaction, as displayed by linear fitting curve of ln(Ao/At) vs light irradiations time Fig. 13 which was additionally assisted by correlation coefficient (R2) data (i.e., 0.95–1.00) [63].

The equation mentioned below are used for first-order reaction and time required for its 50 % completion (t50%) [64]:

where Ao and At are conc. of dye at t = 0 i.e., dye concentration left after adsorption-desorption equilibrium and at time t, sequentially while ‘k’ is reaction rate coefficient that was find out from the slope of Fig. 13. The ‘k’ value for CV dye decontamination was found to 0.05761 min−1. The photocatalyst N-G3h exhibited outstanding decontamination of CV dye with a value of 99.13 % as photocatalyst (Table 7 ). The relation mentioned below has been employed to calculate the % decontamination of CV dye utilizing the as-obtained N-G3h photocatalysts:

In addition, using the equations mentioned below, associated electricity cost (EC) for degradation of CV dye has been determined and tabulated in Table 8 [65,66]:

where P = power of UV source (W), t90% = time needed for 90 % dye eradication (min) and V = dye solution volume (mL).

The photocatalyst will be more economically affordable if its degradation performance is higher since it will require less electricity altogether for the process of dye elimination. The cost of electrical energy for CV dye breakdown reduces with prolongation of pyrolysis period for production of N-C-dots, as previously mentioned in “Section 4.1 - Impact of Pyrolysis Duration on CV Dye Degradation”, the ordering of photocatalytic efficacy for nitrogen-doped-C-dots is N-G3h > N-G2h > N-G1h. This is also illustrated in Fig. 9. Therefore, it is evident that the electrical energy per order decreases with increasing pyrolysis period, reducing the total cost for electricity and positioning N-G3h as the most economical photocatalyst. The as-fabricated N-G3h outperforms the previously reported biomass derived C-dots by degrading greater extent of CV concentration and that too in smaller interval of time (Table 9 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability examination of N-G3h
ce:section.ce:para: To ascertain the possibility of reuse and stability of N-G3h, experiments for both reusability and stability were performed. N-G3h, were used to conduct the reusability test. The sample demonstrated exceptional reusability up to 5 cycles, with just a slight decrement in dye degradation efficacy i.e., 99.13 % degradation efficiency in the first cycle and 86.45 % degradation efficiency in the fifth reusability cycle as illustrated in Fig. 14 (a) [71]. This small reduction the photocatalytic performance of N-G3h is possibly due to continuous weight loss of N-G3h following each reusability cycles. The XRD analysis of fresh N-O3h and following the fifth photocatalytic phase was done to investigate its stability, and the outcomes are displayed in Fig. 14 (b). There is very little variation in XRD spectra of fresh and re-used photocatalyst, indicating excellent N-G3h stability [71,72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CV dye photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
When a suitable light source is present (one whose light energy is either the same or higher as compared to E
g of the photocatalyst), a redox reaction occur caused by photogenerated electrons-holes. [73,74]. Numerous mechanistic pathways have been hypothesized based upon interaction between carbon-dots and dye. Major and the most frequent mechanistic approach used by N-C-dots to photocatalytically decontaminate dye involves the creation of the e−/h+ pair that is aided by the comparatively small Eg and excitation wavelength, which makes it easier for the generation of OH• and O2 • − radicals [75,76].

The several surface functional moieties of C-dots interact with dye molecules to form H-bonding. Upon light irradiations, the N-C-dot surface can prevent e− and h+ from reuniting by capturing electrons and enable efficient charge transfer for the intended photoreactions. Surface functional parts of N-C-dots, which include -OH, -COOH, and -CONH2 via which they effectively form H-bonding connections to the CV dye molecules are probably the primary region for photocatalytic reaction. Moreover, the disengagement of e−-h+ couples may be efficiently enhanced by fast electron transfer among absorbed CV molecules and carbon-dots [16]. Eqs. 8-18 illustrate the prevalent reaction pathway that takes place during the elimination of CV dye by carbon-dots photocatalyst (Fig. 15 and S1) [8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of existence of different scavengers on photocatalytic dye decontamination
ce:section.ce:para: When a photocatalyst is subjected to light irradiation it typically produces 3 kinds of predominantly reactive components in aqueous solution: holes, •O2 − and •OH radicals. These radicals contribute to dye eradication through photocatalysis in an efficient manner [19]. To gain a thorough understanding of aforementioned reactive species' involvement in the photocatalytic processing of dye polluted wastewater, the trapping studies including their entrapment were carried out [77]. An independent capture study was conducted for each of the three scavengers for h+, •OH and •O2 −. 10 mM of each scavenger was used for capture experiment. Na2EDTA was used for the h+ scavenging, 1,4-Benzoquinone (1,4-BZQ/p-BZQ) for the •O2 − scavenging and tertiary-butyl alcohol (t-BA) for the •OH scavenging.

Due to the capture of h+ and •O2 − radicals, correspondingly, it was shown that Na2EDTA and p-BZQ significantly decreased the photocatalytic elimination of CV dye. On the other flip, the inclusion of t-BA for the •OH scavenging resulted in the least reduction in the degradation of CV dye (Fig. 16 ) [78,79]. According to Fig. 16, the inclusion of t-BA, Na2EDTA, and p-BZQ decreased the decontamination percentages to approximately 70 %, 60 %, and 58 %. This indicates that h+ and •O2 − contribute most significantly to the photocatalytic reaction, while •OH is a supplemental species. Accordingly, the following is the order of effective species for photocatalytic decontamination of CV dye: •O2 − > h+ > •OH.

The thorough investigation into how the synthesized N-C-dots affect wheat seed germination process has provided deep insight into the biocompatibility of as-obtained N-C-dots in agricultural fields. The cell wall of wheat seeds is thought to be extremely thin and intensively susceptible to several kinds of contaminants [80]. The mean root length, shoot length, germination percentage, germination index, % inhibitory growth, and Vigor index I of C-dots have been determined right through seed germination process to figure out their major effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact on mean root length and shoot length in presence of as-obtained N-C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
It was noticed that root length of wheat seed increased linearly from day 1 to day 5 of exposure, up to 200 ppm concentration level of N-C-dots (Fig. S2). Furthermore, it has been noticed that the longest root length of any wheat seed found in case of N-G1h followed by N-G2h and N-G3h. It can be concluded that maximum optimum concentration for seed growth is 200 ppm and above which it started decreasing. Decrease in root length at higher concentration can be explained as it has ability to dehydrate seed kernels which produces certain abnormalities in it eventually affecting the germination procedure of seeds [80].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact on % inhibition, seed germination and germination index
ce:section.ce:para: By examining the measurements of the germination index of seedlings in the environment of N-C-dots, it has been feasible to additionally explore the productivity of plants under ideal conditions (Fig. S3). When the concentration of different N-C-dots is changed, it was noticed that germination index values increase linearly. These selected N-C-dots have also been found to be incapable of obstructing the transmembrane proteins that regulate water channels in seeds, which has additionally improved and regulated seed water permeability in seeds. Consequently, the selection of N-C-dots was crucial to the progress of plant germination.

After three days of exposure to wheat at a 200-ppm concentration, N-G1h showed the highest value of the germination index at ~116.9 %, followed by N-G2h and N-G3h. The inhibition of aquaporins in the existence of increasing amounts of C-dots may be the cause of the minor reduction in germination index in concentrations of 300 ppm and above, which ultimately impaired the absorption of water capacity of seeds and reduced their germination index [80]. Order of germination index in the existence of N-C-dots: - N-G1h > N-G2h > N-G3h

According to the computed findings, the value of % inhibition was observed to be negative in all cases at a concentration of 200 ppm, indicating the synthesized N-C-dots' excellent biocompatibility. As the concentration rises from 200 ppm to 400 ppm, the value of PIG reduces. It is clear from the obtained data that the negative value of PIG shows the maximal germination ability and germination index values. The synthesized N-C-dots are assumed to absorb several nutrients ions like K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, etc. and transfer to plant. The interaction of hydrophilic groups of N-C-dots like -OH and -COOH with these metal ions through H-Bonding and electrostatic interaction leads to the adsorption of these metal ions on N-C-dots' surface. Thus, the movement of these nutrients rich N-C-dots into the plant increases the nutrients contents in the plants and consequently boost the seeds germination process. In addition, N-C-dots have an important role in electron transport in photosystem II (PS II) and boost the photosynthesis process that results into greater growth rate of plant as shown in Fig. 17 [81].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Vigor Index I in presence of N-C-dots (N-G1h, N-G2h, N-G3h)
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of seeds to produce normal seedlings have been mainly impacted by the presence of N-C-dots in their environment which mainly affects the rate of germination and mean length of seedlings. As, the rate of germination was determined to be 100 % in cases all N-C-dots, so only the mean root length has impacted the Vigor index of selected seeds.

Maximum growth of root was found to be different in different doped-C-dots', e.g., N-G1h, N-G2h, N-G3h. In case of wheat, N-G1h demonstrated highest Vigor index value of 152 in comparison to that of N-G2h and N-G3h as followed by N-G2h > N-G3h at 200 ppm of concentration (see Fig. S4). Increase in Vigor index indicates the absorption of more water molecules by plants and their enhanced growth [82]. Furthermore, it has been observed that N-G1h showed enhanced biocompatibility more than the other N-C-dots. The maximum germination of wheat was observed at 200 ppm concentration of N-C-dots. For each kind of seed and at all concentration levels, the percentage of inhibition growth have been observed to be negligible, demonstrating the outstanding biocompatibility of the synthesized N-C-dots. Therefore, the resulting N-C-dots are biocompatible and non-toxic in nature which can further be used in agricultural field.

Garlic (Allium Sativum) mediated N-doped-C-dots were fabricated via a facile and cost-efficient pyrolysis route with varying pyrolysis time. The extent of crystallinity of synthesized N-C-dots increased by prolongation pyrolysis as evident from the XRD analysis. The biological compatibility was studied from the impact of various concentrations of synthesized N-C-dots on the wheat roots during the germination process and all the N-C-dots were found to be biocompatible. N-C-dots exhibited outstanding photocatalytic decolorization and decontamination of CV dye, and various statistics including rate constant, electrical energy per order, percentage CV dye degradation, half-life, and electricity expenses have been determined. In addition, various parameters like pyrolysis time, photocatalyst amount, dye concentration, pH, light source distance and dye containing beaker diameter have been optimized. The rate of CV degradation was observed to rise with increasing pyrolysis duration of the synthesized C-dots. The increase in the overall surface area along with number of surface-active functional groups with longer pyrolysis interval is attributed to the enhanced photocatalytic performance of N-G3h which degraded 40 ppm CV dye to the maximum extent (99.13 %) within 80 min. Hence, it may be inferred from the present work that photocatalytic activities of doped-C-dots can be improved with longer pyrolysis time and may be extremely useful for remediation of CV dye contaminated wastewater.
